items.a_description: A curated collection of web design styles. Each page is presented entirely in the aesthetic it describes — the design is the content.
Design styles that draw from past decades — from 70s earth tones to 90s web chaos to Y2K shimmer — reimagined for the modern screen.

items.title: 70s Design
items.description: Harvest gold, avocado green, and burnt orange on warm cream — the unmistakable palette of the 1970s. This aesthetic draws from the era of sunken living rooms, macrame wall hangings, and vinyl record sleeves, with heavy slab-serif letterforms and a sunburst motif that signals the decade's love of bold decorative geometry.

items.title: 80s Design
items.description: 80s design is the visual language of neon on black — glowing magenta headlines, electric cyan grid floors receding into a dark horizon, and an overall feeling that every surface is emitting light rather than reflecting it.

items.title: 90s Web Revival
items.description: 90s Web Revival reconstructs the raw, exuberant visual language of the early commercial internet — tiled background textures, beveled push-buttons straight out of Windows 95, pixel-font headings, and that unmistakable sense that someone had just discovered the blink tag.

items.title: Cassette Mixtape UI
items.description: The handmade warmth of a cassette tape label — Sharpie lettering on a creamy sticker, faint ruled lines, and the slightly crooked, deeply personal aesthetic of a mixtape made for someone you care about.

items.a_description: Web design shaped by geography and tradition — from Japanese density to Scandinavian minimalism to Swiss precision.

items.title: Chinese Web Design
items.description: Bold reds, golden accents, dense information grids, and the unmistakable energy of a digital culture built around commerce and connection — where visual boldness signals trustworthiness and content density signals thoroughness.

items.title: Dutch Design
items.description: Bold primary colors, sharp geometric forms, confident typography, and the playful-yet-structured spirit of a nation that gave the world De Stijl and Mondrian — centuries of graphic excellence translated into digital form.

items.title: Japandi
items.description: The quiet harmony of Japanese minimalism meeting Scandinavian warmth — unbleached linen tones, organic textures, and the meditative calm of a space where every element has been considered and most have been gently removed.

items.title: Japanese Web Design
items.description: Dense grids of information, tiny text packed into every available pixel, bright red accent labels, and the methodical organization of a culture that values thoroughness over minimalism.

items.title: Korean Web Design
items.description: Soft coral accents, rounded card surfaces, and the polished elegance of interfaces designed for a culture that invented K-beauty — tech-forward cleanliness with a warmer, more approachable sensibility.

items.title: Scandinavian Nordic Design
items.description: Pure white space, muted natural tones, and the radical simplicity of design that believes less is always more — functional minimalism of Copenhagen, Stockholm, and Helsinki brought to the screen.

items.title: Swiss Graphic Style
items.description: Radical clarity through mathematical precision — sans-serif type, strict grid alignment, generous whitespace, and a single bold accent color against monochrome. Impeccable typography, systematic grids, and disciplined restraint.

items.title: Wabi-Sabi
items.description: Cracked pottery mended with gold, weathered wood smoothed by years of touch, and the quiet beauty of impermanence — nothing is perfectly centered, nothing is too polished, and nothing tries too hard.

items.a_description: Timeless art movements translated into digital interfaces — from Art Deco geometry to Bauhaus functionalism.

items.title: Art Deco
items.description: Elegant, geometric, angular elements combined to create interfaces that are immediately recognizable and purposefully designed — every color, font choice, and spacing decision evokes the specific mood of the era.

items.title: Art Nouveau
items.description: Organic, elegant, flowing elements that create interfaces where every curve and color choice evokes the specific mood and cultural context of this timeless aesthetic.

items.title: Bauhaus
items.description: Geometric forms, grid-based layouts, and professional restraint — the visual language of Bauhaus where form follows function and every element serves a purpose.

items.title: Constructivism
items.description: Bold, angular, geometric elements creating interfaces with the revolutionary energy and purposeful design of 1920s Russian avant-garde.
